sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Causal Transformer language models suffer from strictly sequential decoding and a quadratic per-step attention cost. While linear-time causal models and discrete diffusion models each address these weaknesses, their integration remains inherently inconsistent: diffusion requires bidirectional attention, while causal models are unidirectional. To unify these architectures, we propose $B^3D-RWKV$, a diffusion RWKV variant that integrates the model's $O(L)$ inference efficiency with parallel, bidirectional discrete-diffusion through a \emph{triplet-block layout} method. $B^3D-RWKV-7.2B$ reaches comparable accuracy on an 8-task suite versus existing models while significantly outperforming baselines in decoding throughput with an average of $\mathbf{1.6\times}$ speedup.

sections.content: 6 2 0 2 5 2 ] . [ 1 9 6 9 5 2 . 5 0 6 2 : r Triplet-Block Diffusion RWKV Ke Lin* William & Mary leonard.keilin@gmail.com Yiyang Luo* HKUST yluodq@connect.ust.hk Zhaolong Su Cornell zs494@cornell.edu Yunya Song HKUST yunyasong@ust.hk

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Causal Transformer language models suffer from strictly sequential decoding and quadratic per-step attention cost. While lineartime causal models and discrete diffusion models each address these weaknesses, their integration remains inherently inconsistent: diffusion requires bidirectional attention, while causal models are unidirectional. To unify these architectures, we propose B3D-RWKV, diffusion RWKV variant that integrates the models O(L) inference efficiency with parallel, bidirectional discrete-diffusion through triplet-block layout method. B3D-RWKV7.2B reaches comparable accuracy on an 8task suite versus existing models while significantly outperforming baselines in decoding throughput with an average of 1.6 speedup. Code is available at https://github.com/ leonardodalinky/B3D-RWKV.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Large language models (LLMs) have advanced rapidly under the dominance of the strictly causal Transformer architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017), yet the left-to-right design of most modern decoders introduces two structural limitations: sequential decoding, which prevents parallelization, and quadratic attention costs, which make longcontext inference expensive. These drawbacks have driven the development of alternative architectures designed to challenge the Transformers dominance: (1) Discrete-diffusion language models (Nie et al., 2025; Bie et al., 2025; Ye et al., 2025; Gong et al., 2024) avoid sequential decoding, instead denoising token blocks in parallel using bidirectional attention (Arriola et al., 2025). (2) The RWKV family (Peng et al., 2023, 2024, 2025) reformulates the classical Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) with attention-like channel mixing to obtain O(L) inference at Transformer-level quality. *Equal Contribution. 1 Anyi Rao HKUST anyirao@ust.hk This motivates us to combine these alternative architectures to improve generation efficiency over standard Transformers. However, using strictly causal backbone for diffusion language models presents an architectural mismatch: diffusion requires bidirectional attention, while causal models are unidirectional. To achieve this combination, we introduce triplet-block layout method that converts causal RNN-style language model into block-diffusion language model without altering the backbone. Each logical generation block of size appears three times consecutively in training sample: masked copy b1, an identical masked copy b2 on which the denoising loss is computed, and clean ground-truth copy b3 that refreshes the recurrent state before the next block. Because the backbone model reads strictly left-to-right, the hidden state arriving at any masked position of b2 has already absorbed every unmasked token of b1, so b2 gains pseudo-bidirectional access to its own unmasked context on strictly causal model. Our contributions are as follows: We release B3D-RWKV-7.2B, the first diffusionstyle linear-time RNN language model trained at the 7B scale with the mask-prediction objective. We train the model using our triplet-block diffusion framework, which integrates parallel token selection into the RWKV-7 backbone without modifying its original parameters. We provide comprehensive comparison between our model and other strictly causal language models on an 8-task suite. We also demonstrate that our 7.2B model matches the reasoning capabilities of the RWKV-7 baseline while achieving 1.6 the decoding throughput at comparable generation lengths. Figure 1: (a) Triplet-block layout for diffusion training on strictly causal LM. Each logical block unfolds left to right as three contiguous physical blocks: masked copy b(i) 2 , and clean ground-truth copy b(i) that refreshes the recurrent hidden state for block + 1. (b) Per-block iterative denoising at 3 inference. At each step, the sampler commits every position whose top-1 probability exceeds τ . The loop terminates when every position is committed; the now-clean block is appended to c, and the next logical block begins. 1 , an identical lossable masked copy b(i)

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Discrete-diffusion and masked language models. The thread traces back to BERT-style maskedlanguage pretraining (Devlin et al., 2019) and Mask-Predicts parallel decoder (Ghazvininejad et al., 2019), which MaskGIT (Chang et al., 2022) carried to image transformers with confidencethresholded commit schedule that almost every The discretelater masked generator reuses. diffusion family proper was introduced by D3PM (Austin et al., 2021), with SEDD (Lou et al., 2023), MDLM (Sahoo et al., 2024), and MD4 (Shi et al., 2024) reformulating and simplifying the absorbingstate objective. More recent scaled-up systems, including LLaDA (Nie et al., 2025), LLaDA 2.x (Bie et al., 2025), Dream 7B (Ye et al., 2025), DiffuLLaMA (Gong et al., 2024), Block Diffusion (Arriola et al., 2025), WeDLM (Liu et al., 2025), and Nemotron-Labs-Diffusion (Fu et al., 2026), combine these objectives with instruction tuning and parallel decoding. The concurrent DiffuMamba (Singh et al., 2026) is the closest design point to ours and the only prior recipe that pairs maskeddiffusion objective with linear-time backbone, but it does so by architecturally modifying Mamba into bidirectional block and so trains from scratch at the 1.3B scale on DCLM (Li et al., 2024). Linear-time recurrent and state-space backbones. parallel thread has produced strictly causal, linear-time alternatives to softmax attention: the RWKV family from RWKV-4 (Peng et al., 2023) through Eagle/Finch (Peng et al., 2024) to RWKV-7 (Peng et al., 2025); the selective state-space models (SSM) Mamba and Mamba-2 (Gu and Dao, 2023; Dao and Gu, 2024); RetNet (Sun et al., 2023); Gated Linear Attention (Yang et al., 2023); and the Hyena Hierarchy (Poli et al., 2023). These backbones report perplexity parity with quadratic-attention Transformers at large wallclock and memory savings; to our knowledge, none have been combined with discrete-diffusion training objective at large scale.

sections.title: 3 Method
sections.content: To enable diffusion paradigm within strictly causal language models, we propose triplet-block layout for efficient training and inference. This method comprises triplet-block layout (3.1) and blockwise iterative denoising sampler (3.2). Implementation details of training and inference are provided in Appendix A.1 and A.2. 3.1 Triplet-block layout Let the training context length be L, and the logical generation block size be B. We partition each training sample into = L/B contiguous logical blocks. For each logical block index {1, . . . , }, denote the clean ground-truth tokens by g(i) B. Each logical block is then laid out as the concatenation of three physical blocks of 2 Model LLaMA3-8B Qwen3-8B RWKV-7-7.2B LLaDA-8B Dream-7B DiffuMamba B3D-RWKV-7.2B General Tasks Math & Science MMLU(5) ARC-C(0) ARC-E(0) PIQA(0) RACE(0) GSM8K(8) MATH(4) GPQA(5) 66.6 76.9 65.1 65.9 69.5 - 64.8 53.6 56.6 55.5 47.5 59.8 28.3 61. Causal LM 79.8 79.1 80.7 81.1 81.7 83.8 Diffusion LM 74.8 75.8 71.8 83.9 Strictly Causal Diffusion LM 49.1 79. 62.6 73.5 41.9 - 43.5 38.7 44.7 - 49.7 78.9 89.9 83.9 70.9 77. - 71.5 41.1 60.8 48.8 30.7 39.6 - 23.8 35.5 44.4 30.8 30.4 36. - 25.6 Table 1: Benchmark results on the 8-task suite. The number of few-shot examples for each benchmark is indicated in brackets. Results outperforming the RWKV baseline are bolded, while comparable results are underlined. length (Fig. 1(a): b(i) 1 (cid:124)(cid:123)(cid:122)(cid:125) masked copy b(i) 2 (cid:124)(cid:123)(cid:122)(cid:125) masked copy (lossable) b(i) 3 (cid:124)(cid:123)(cid:122)(cid:125) clean copy . (1) 1 and b(i) The two masked copies b(i) 2 are identical: they share the same mask pattern m(i) {0, 1}B, replacing masked positions with [MASK] and retaining g(i) elsewhere. The clean copy b(i) 3 is also identical to g(i). Let ℓ(i) {0, 1} be the lossable flag, π(i, j) the physical position of the j-th token of b(i) 2 , and pij() pθ( x<π(i,j)) the next-token distribution there. Writing = {(i, j) : m(i) = 1} for the supervised positions and Nv = S, the training loss is the mean cross-entropy on S: ℓ(i) LCE(θ) = (cid:88) log pij (cid:0)g(i) (cid:1). (2) 1 Nv (i,j)S Following the Confidence-Aware Parallel training scheme of LLaDA-2.0 (Bie et al., 2025), we further sharpen pij on supervised positions that are already correctly predicted, so that the inference-time threshold sampler (3.2) can commit more positions per denoising step. Let ˆg(i) = arg maxv pij(v) be the models current top-1, H(p) = (cid:80) p(v) log p(v) the entropy, = {(i, j) : ˆg(i) = g(i) } the gated subset, and Nc = C: LCAP(θ) = (cid:88) H(cid:0)pij (cid:1). (3) 1 Nc (i,j)C The membership in is computed without gradient, so the entropy flows only on the selected subset. The total objective is L(θ) = LCE(θ) + λCAP LCAP(θ). (4) 3 1 . Because b(i) Pseudo-bidirectional access. Fix any masked position with block-local index {0, . . . , 1} in b(i) 2 , whose physical position π(i, j) sits after every token of b(i) 1 . Two complementary streams of context are visible there. (i) Left context. Within b(i) 2 itself, the unmasked tokens at indices < lie to the left of π(i, j) and supply the standard causal left context that vanilla decoder would use. (ii) Right context via b(i) 1 has been processed in full before π(i, j) and carries the same mask pattern m(i), its unmasked tokens at every block-local index are already absorbed into the hidden state at π(i, j). The union of streams (i) and (ii) is exactly the set of unmasked tokens of the logical block, so position receives full bidirectional conditioning over block while the backbone still reads the sample strictly left-to-right  (Fig. 1)  . Although the training context is 3 the original length, the architecture of strictly causal models remains more computationally efficient than standard attention-based models. Requirements and universal claims. The construction in Eq. (2) and the pseudo-bidirectionalaccess argument depend on only two properties of the backbone pθ: (R1) Strict causality: the predictive distribution at any position depends solely on positions strictly to its left. (R2) Forward-propagating state: an internal state that allows the predictive distribution at positions 2 to access unmasked tokens from b(i) b(i) 1 . Every member of the linear-time backbone family currently in use, such as RWKV-v4 through v7, Mamba and Mamba-2, RetNet, Gated Linear Attention, and Hyena, satisfies (R1) and (R2) by construction. Standard causal Transformers also satisfy these, but their triple sequence-length cost is unattractive. Therefore, the triplet construction defines universally no-architectural-change training recipe over the class of strictly causal backbones. 3.2 Inference: block-wise iterative denoising At inference, the model generates one logical block at time with only 2 replicates of physical blocks. Let denote the prefix of already-committed tokens. For each new block, the sampler initializes to an all-MASK input of length and runs at most denoising iterations. At each iteration the sampler forwards concatenated with the current best guess of the block, reads the top-1 probability pj at each still-masked position, and commits any position with pj > τ ; low-confidence fallback commits the top-kmin positions whenever fewer than kmin clear the threshold, guaranteeing strictly positive progress per iteration. Appendix A.2 records the per-iteration loop and Figure 1 summarizes it.

sections.title: 4 Experiments
sections.content: 4.1 Setup The backbone is the public RWKV-7-g1f-7.2B (Peng et al., 2025) causal-LM checkpoint. Training data is the mixture of TÜLU 3 SFT dataset (Lambert et al., 2024) and curated trajectories of GLM5.1 and Claude Opus 4.6. In the first training round, we set the triplet layout (3.1) to = 32 and = 64, expanding 2,048-token samples into 6,144-token sequences for 1.8 epochs. In the second round, we increase the layout to = 256, expanding 8,192-token samples into 24,576-token sequences for 0.2 epochs. The λCAP is set to 0.5. The model is trained on 8 H100 80GB SXM GPUs. The full setup is in Appendix and B. 4.2 Benchmarks We evaluate B3D-RWKV-7.2B on an 8-task suite: MMLU (Hendrycks et al., 2020), ARC-Challenge, ARC-Easy (Clark et al., 2018), PIQA (Bisk et al., 2019), RACE (Lai et al., 2017), GSM8K (Cobbe et al., 2021) and MATH (Hendrycks et al., 2021) and GPQA (Rein et al., 2023). For fair comparison, we restrict baselines to backbones of comparable parameter scale released in roughly the same time window as RWKV-7. Table 1 presents downstream performance on general and math reasoning tasks. B3D-RWKV performs comparably to other diffusion LMs of similar scale and matches the performance of the RWKV-7 Figure 2: Inference throughput of LLaDA-8B, RWKV7.2B, and B3D-RWKV-7.2B on an H100 80GB GPU. baseline. Notably, our method outperforms others on benchmarks like ARC-C and RACE, likely due to pseudo-bidirectional perception-enhancing reasoning capabilities. Conversely, parallel decoding may slightly reduce math reasoning accuracy, as these problems involve highly complex structures. For example, MATH is graded by LaTeXlevel answer verifier that demands exact symbolic and numerical agreement, leaving no partial credit for minor local errors, which is exactly the failure mode that parallel decoding is most exposed to. These results show that B3D-RWKV achieves comparable or superior performance on simpler tasks, but experiences an acceptable drop on complex structural problems, likely due to parallel decoding issues of diffusion language models. 4.3 Throughput Figure 2 compares the inference throughput of LLaDA-8B and our model against an RWKV-7 baseline across context lengths from 1K to 512K. LLaDA-8B utilizes Fast-dllm (Wu et al., 2026) for efficient inference, with batch size fixed at 1, block size = 32, and = 32 diffusion steps. The commit threshold is set to 0.9 in our settings. Our model consistently achieves an average of 1.6 higher throughput than RWKV-7 while maintaining nearly identical performance. Adjusting sampling parameters achieves 2.02 speedup with slight drop in quality. More details of throughput is in Appendix C.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: We propose triplet-block layout training method to adapt strictly causal language models into generative diffusion language models, and it requires no architectural changes. B3D-RWKV achieves 4 general 1.6 throughput compared to the original RWKV model, while maintaining comparable performance to existing models, offering an efficient way to transform pre-trained causal language models into diffusion language models.

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: Universality argued structurally; demonstrated on one backbone. The universality claim is structural: the architectural requirements (R1, R2 in 3.1) are stated precisely, and every member of the linear-time backbone family we cite satisfies them by construction. Due to computational constraints, we empirically validate the recipe using single 7.2B-parameter RWKV-7 backbone. Empirical confirmation on smaller RWKV-v7 checkpoints, on other RWKV variants (Peng et al., 2023, 2024), and on non-RWKV linear-time backbones, such as Mamba, is left to future work. 3 physical-sequence cost. The triplet layout applies multiplicative 3 factor to the physical sequence length per logical block in exchange for pseudo-bidirectional access. RWKV-7s linearin-length complexity (Peng et al., 2025) makes this approach feasible, whereas the quadratic cost of Transformers has previously restricted discretediffusion training to short context lengths. Small-scale SFT data and no RL alignment. We continued training using only the TÜLU 3 SFT mixture and curated set of reasoning trajectories (Section 2), totaling 4.9B tokens. Additionally, we run neither large-scale further pretraining nor any subsequent reinforcement-learning alignment stage. Compared with the trillion-token corpus that produced the parent RWKV-7 Goose checkpoint, this is narrow and stylistically biased distribution, so degree of catastrophic forgetting on capabilities the base model originally acquired from broad pretrain data is essentially unavoidable, and likely accounts for part of the accuracy regression we observe on subset of evaluation tasks. We expect that scaling the diffusion-style post-training corpus and adding an RL alignment stage on top of B3D-RWKV would recover, and likely exceed, the parent checkpoints accuracy on the affected tasks; both are left to future work. coding. However, the pretrained RWKVs inherent capabilities allow for success in some simple coding tasks, as demonstrated in Appendix D. We intend to improve this in future work.

sections.title: Ethical Considerations
sections.content: B3D-RWKV is initialized from publicly released causal language model, and our fine-tuning only changes its architectural behavior, not its safety properties. We did not run any additional alignment or content filtering, so B3D-RWKV inherits whatever biases and inaccuracies already exist in its base checkpoint and pre-training corpus. We recommend reviewing its outputs before using B3DRWKV in any user-facing system or in settings where factual accuracy matters. Furthermore, the training dataset is publicly available and we do not impose any security check upon them.

sections.title: A Model Implementation
sections.content: A.1 Mask Sampling Rules Following LLaDA (Nie et al., 2025), each logical block independently samples mask ratio r(i) Uniform[0, 1], then draws r(i)B mask positions uniformly without replacement from the lossable subset; with probability 0.10 the ratio is overridden to 1.0 (full-mask augmentation) so the training distribution matches the inference distribution, where each new block begins fully masked. Two role-aware rules are necessary and we treat force-mask EOS (the them as non-negotiable: document-final EOS is in every samples mask set) and force-mask PAD inside the EOS-containing block (trailing PAD positions are extended to the mask). Both supervise the stopping decision and block trailing-PAD shortcut that does not transfer to inference. Per-block uniform mask ratio. Following LLaDA (Nie et al., 2025), each logical block independently samples its mask ratio r(i) Uniform[rmin, rmax] with rmin = 0 and rmax = 1 in all our runs. The mask pattern m(i) {0, 1}B is then drawn by sampling r(i)B positions uniformly without replacement from the lossable subset of block i. Full-mask trick. To shrink the distribution gap between training and inference, where each new block begins fully masked, we override r(i) to 1.0 with probability 0.10 at sample time, so roughly one in ten logical blocks has every lossable position masked. This is the LLaDA full-mask augmentation (Nie et al., 2025) applied per-block rather than per-sample, and it is the only training-time hyperparameter we vary on the mask-sampling axis. Force-mask EOS. Every document carries document-final EOS token at g(i) for some (i, j), where is the EOS-containing logical block. We force-include this position in m(i) in every sample of every epoch. Without this rule, uniform perblock mask ratio of Uniform[0, 1] supervises the EOS position in fewer than half of all samples and contributes only small fraction of the total loss signal. Qualitatively, an unsupervised EOS produces an inference-time model that never stops generating, while force-masking the EOS yields well-calibrated stopping behavior. Force-mask PAD inside the EOS-containing block. document shorter than tokens leaves tail of PAD symbols inside the EOS-containing logical block. Without intervention, the model learns the shortcut MASK followed by visible PAD implies EOS, cue that does not transfer to inference because PAD is suppressed in the decoder. We therefore extend the forced mask to all PAD positions in the EOS-containing block. The model is then forced to predict the document-final EOS from the upstream content rather than from trailingPAD shortcut. Vocabulary slot reuse. The RWKV-world tokenizer (Peng et al., 2025) uses 65,530 real token slots out of its 65,536-padded embedding table. We re-purpose two of the unused trailing slots, setting ID 65,535 as MASK and ID 65,534 as PAD, while preserving ID 0 as the original EOS. The embedding table and output projection are therefore not extended; no new parameters are introduced for the diffusion training objective. A.2 Inference Sampler This appendix expands the per-iteration commit rule of 3.2. Per-iteration commit rule. At each iteration t, the sampler forwards concatenated with the current best guess of the block through the backbone, reads the top-1 probability pj at each stillmasked position j, and commits any position whose pj exceeds fixed confidence threshold τ . If fewer than floor kmin positions are committed in iteration t, the sampler falls back to committing the top-kmin most confident positions even when they sit below τ . This guarantees strictly positive progress per iteration. The rule is the LLaDA 2.0 (Bie et al., 2025) confidence-thresholdplus-low-confidence-fallback rule, which itself descends from the MaskGIT (Chang et al., 2022) confidence-thresholded commit schedule for nonautoregressive masked generators. Iteration + 1 then conditions on the newly-committed tokens. The loop exits as soon as every position in the block is committed; the now-clean block is appended to c, and the next logical block begins. A.3 Architecture and Model Layout Table 2 lists the architectural configuration of B3D-RWKV-7.2B and the corresponding physicalsequence layout induced by the triplet block training scheme. All values match the public RWKV7G1f-7.2B checkpoint we initialize from; we do not modify the backbone architecture. 8 Figure 3: Effect of (a) sampling steps and (b) commit threshold τ on decoding throughput and accuracy on ARC-E benchmark. Metric Layers Embedding dim Head size Number of heads RWKV LoRA rank Total parameters Vocabulary slots (padded) Vocabulary slots (used) MASK token ID PAD token ID Context length (training) Block size Logical blocks per sample Raw content len. per sample Value 32 4,096 64 64 128/128/96/480 7.2 65,536 65,530 65,535 65,534 6,144 32 64 2,048 Training Dataset. We trained the model on 4.97 billion tokens mixture of the allenai/tulu-3sft-mixture, Jackrong/GLM-5.1-Reasoning-1MCleaned, and angrygiraffe/claude-opus-4.6-4.7reasoning-8.7k datasets on Huggingface Datasets. Precision and gradient accumulation. The run uses bf16 mixed-precision activations and fp32 master copy of the optimizer state. Gradient accumulation precision must be set explicitly to fp32 via the DeepSpeed configuration key data_types.grad_accum_dtype = fp32. The Adam optimizer (Kingma and Ba, 2014) master weights, first-moment buffer, and secondmoment buffer are all fp32 inside DeepSpeeds BF16_Optimizer. Table 2: Architecture and physical layout of B3DRWKV-7.2B. The triplet layout maps each 2,048-token raw content sample to 6,144-token physical sequence consisting of 64 logical blocks of size = 32, each unfolded as three contiguous physical blocks (b1, b2, b3).

sections.title: B Implementation Notes
sections.content: This appendix records the precise distributedtraining configuration used by our single B3DRWKV-7.2B run, for reproducibility. The notes here are not claimed as contribution; they allow exact reproduction of the training run and flag two configuration pitfalls that are easy to overlook in the DeepSpeed (Rajbhandari et al., 2019) + PyTorch-Lightning stack. Training takes total of approximately 500 H100 hours using 8 H100 80GB SXM GPUs to complete two epochs. uses communication communication precision. Inter-rank via fp32 Inter-rank communication_data_type = fp32 in the DeepSpeed configuration. Default communication- (allgather_bucket_size, bucket their 200 reduce_bucket_size) are kept at MB defaults; reducing them traded fragmentation for throughput in our exploratory tests. sizes clipping. The PyTorch-Lightning Gradient Trainers argument gradient_clip_val is silently ignored on the DeepSpeed strategy path. The same value must be propagated through the strategys configuration as strategy.config[gradient_clipping] for it to take effect; we set this to 0.5 matching the Trainer-side value, so both code paths agree. Adam optimizer epsilon. We set the Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2014) optimizer epsilon to ϵ = 108. The upstream RWKV training-code default 9 Component Approx. peak (GB) Avg Std Min Max Model weights (bf16) Gradients (bf16, ZeRO-2) Adam state (fp32) Activations CUDA context overhead Total observed 14.4 1.8 10.8 30 1.5 5860 Table 3: Single-GPU peak memory footprint (trainingtime) for the post-fix B3D-RWKV-7.2B configuration on an H100 80GB. Activations and the wkv scratchpad dominate the budget. of 1018 is intended for the small-scale fp32 setting and is not appropriate for the GPU FusedAdam kernel that DeepSpeeds BF16 Optimizer dispatches to in our configuration; we therefore raise it to 108, the standard Adam default. Parallelism. We run DeepSpeed (Rajbhandari et al., 2019) ZeRO Stage 2 w/o offload on 8 NVIDIA H100 80GB GPUs. Effective batch size is 4 batches per GPU 8 GPUs 4 gradientaccumulation steps = 128 samples per step. Memory footprint (Training). Table 3 reports the observed single-GPU peak memory split for the post-fix configuration. The activation plus wkvscratchpad budget dominates and is the binding constraint for raising the micro-batch beyond 4 at the current context length. Open-source License. We follow the Apache2.0 License from RWKV directly.

sections.title: C Performance
sections.content: Throughput (Training). On 8 H100 80GB SXM GPUs, the post-fix training configuration sustains approximately 43,000 tokens per second at micro-batch 4 and context length 6,144. Throughput (Inference). Across = 6,284 requests sampled from production-style workload, the mean decoding throughput is 222.1 tok/s, with per-request rates ranging from 74.7 to 785.4 tok/s  (Table 4)  . The large standard deviation (124.6, about 56% of the mean) comes mostly from differences in prompt length and in how many tokens the speculative decoder commits per draft step. Throughput 222.1 124.6 74. 785.4 Table 4: Per-request decoding throughput statistics over = 6284 requests (tokens/second). Figure 4: Latency in milliseconds by prefilled context length. An increase in context length produces corresponding linear increase in latency, which is what we expect from the RWKV-7 backbone since it has no quadratic attention to dominate at long context. Sampling steps. More sampling steps trade throughput for accuracy (Fig. 3 (a)). At 8 steps, the model runs at 581 tok/s but only reaches 18.7% accuracy; at 32 steps, accuracy climbs to 79.3% while throughput drops to 213 tok/s. The largest single jump in accuracy happens between 16 and 24 steps (+30.8 points for 26% throughput drop), which is roughly the point at which iterative denoising starts to produce coherent output. Commit threshold. The commit threshold τ controls how confident the model must be before committing draft token. At τ = 0.3 the decoder commits aggressively and reaches 772 tok/s, but accuracy collapses to 11.2% (Fig. 3 (b)). Raising τ to 0.9 recovers 79.3% accuracy at 213 tok/s, which is the same operating point reached by 32 sampling steps. Both knobs move along the same speedaccuracy frontier; we use τ = 0.9 as the default and treat lower values as tuning knob when latency matters more than accuracy.

sections.title: D Samples
sections.content: Latency (Inference). End-to-end latency grows close to linearly with the prefilled context length, from 91 ms at 1K tokens to 45.8 at 512K  (Fig. 4)  . We include several samples generated by B3DRWKV-7.2B in the Appendix to demonstrate the models proficiency in solving both general and 10 complex tasks. The default system prompt is the standard You are helpful assistant.

sections.title: E Declaration of AI Usage
sections.content: We use Grammarly and Gemini to proofread our papers, and Claude Code for coding. 11 Sample: Knowledge User: Could you tell me the life story of Albert Einstein? Assistant: <think>The user is asking for detailed biography of Albert Einsteins life. This is straightforward request. should cover his early life, his education, his scientific contributions, his challenges, and his later years. need to present this in clear, engaging way. Let me think about what aspects of Einsteins life are most interesting. His early life in Munich, his time in Zurich, his work on the Einstein equation, his struggles with quantum mechanics, and his Nobel Prize are all key points. should also mention his later work on unified field theory. should avoid getting bogged down in the technical details of his equations and focus on the human story. The tension of his relationship with quantum mechanics is great story to include, as it shows his character and the limits of his work. Ill structure this chronologically, starting from his birth in 1879 and going to his death in 1955. Ill include key events like his marriage, the Nobel Prize, and his later work. </think> Albert Einstein (1879-1955) was one of the most influential physicists of the 20th century. Born in Munich, Germany, on March 14, 1879, he was the son of Jewish family. His father, Hermann, was successful manufacturer of electrical goods, and his mother, Pauline, was seammaker. Einstein showed an interest in mathematics from young age. Sample: Biology User: To prevent desiccation and injury, the embryos of terrestrial vertebrates are encased within fluid secreted by the: A. Amnion, B. Chorion, C. Allantois, D. Yolk sac. Which is the right answer? Assistant: <think>The user is asking about the embryonic sacs in terrestrial vertebrates. The correct answer is A. Amnion. Let me think about this carefully. In terrestrial vertebrates, the embryo is enclosed in the amniotic sac, which contains the amniotic fluid. The amniotic fluid is produced by the amnion. The chorrion is the outermost layer of the embryonic sac, and the allantois is the waste absorption sac. The yolk sac is for nutrient absorption. The chorrion is the outermost layer of the amniotic sac, and the fluid is the amniotic sac. The amnion is the membrane that produces the amniotic fluid. The chorrion is the outermost layer of the amniotic sac. The allantois is sac that develops from the embryo. So, the amnion is the structure that secretes the amniotic fluid. </think> The correct answer is **A. Amnion**. 12 Sample: Math User: Joy can read 8 pages of book in 20 minutes. How many hours will it take her to read 120 pages? Assistant: <think>The user wants to find out how many hours it will take Joy to read 120 pages. Joy reads 8 pages in 20 minutes. First, find the number of pages she reads per minute. Total pages = 8 pages in 20 minutes. Pages per minute = 8 pages / 20 minutes = 0.4 pages per minute. Next, find the number of minutes needed to read 120 pages. Total pages = 120 pages. Pages per minute = 8 pages. Total minutes = 120 pages / 8 pages/minute = 15 minutes. Then, convert minutes to hours. 15 minutes = 15 / 60 hours = 0.25 hours. Let me double check. Pages per minute = 8 / 20 = 0.4 Minutes per hour = 120 / 0.4 = 15 minutes. Hours = 15 / 60 = 0.25 hours. Alternatively: Pages per hour = 8 / 20 = 0.4 pages per minute. Pages per hour = 8 / 20 = 0.4 pages per minute. Pages per hour = 0.4 * 60 = 24 pages per hour. Total hours = 120 / 24 = 5 hours. Both methods yield 5 hours. </think> It will take Joy **5** hours to read 120 pages. Sample: Commonsense User: Where is the capital of US? Assistant: <think>The user is asking for the capital of the United States. This is straightforward geography question. </think> The capital of the United States is Washington, D.C. Sample: Translation User: Translate the following sentence to English: Hoy hace muy buen tiempo. Assistant: <think>The user wants me to translate Spanish sentence into English. The sentence is "Hoy hace muy bu tiempo." "Hoy" = today, "hace" = makes, "muy buen" = very good, "tiempo" = "Hoy" = today, "weather" = weather Translation: "It makes very good weather today." </think> "It makes very good weather today. Sample: Multi-turn User: Calculate the result of 13 + 17. Assistant: The answer is **30**. User: Now multiply the previous result by 3. Assistant: <think>The user wants me to multiply the previous result by 3. The previous result was 30. 30 * 3 = 90. </think> The answer is **90**. Sample: Coding User: Write python function to calculate Fibonacci numbers. Assistant: <think> We need to write Python function to calculate Fibonacci numbers. The user says "Write Python function to calculate Fibonacci numbers." That's straightforward. But we need to consider the context: The user likely wants function that returns the nth Fibonacci number. Possibly they want an iterative or recursive approach. Since it's simple request, we can provide function that computes the nth Fibonacci number efficiently. We'll handle edge cases: n=0 returns 0, n=1 returns 1, maybe negative numbers? Typically Fibonacci is defined for non-negative integers. We'll assume is non-negative integer. We'll implement iterative for efficiency. We should output the code. The instruction: "Write Python function to calculate Fibonacci numbers." So we just need to provide the function. Possibly include docstring and example usage. We'll write: def fibonacci(n): if < 0: raise ValueError("n must be non-negative") a, = 0, 1 for _ in range(n): a, = b, + return That's it. </think> ```python def fibonacci(n: int) -> int: """Return the nth Fibonacci number. The Fibonacci sequence is defined as: F(0) = 0 F(1) = 1 F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2) for >= 1 Args: (int): non-negative integer representing the position in the sequence. Returns: int: The nth Fibonacci number. Raises: ValueError: If is negative.""" if < 0: raise ValueError("n must be non-negative") a, = 0, 1 for _ in range(n): a, = b, + return ```
